overview.title: Data Science Tools & Libraries
overview.description: Essential tools and libraries for data analysis, visualization, statistical computing, and data manipulation in Python, R, and interactive environments

resources.name: Pandas - Data Manipulation
resources.shortDesc: Powerful Python library for data manipulation and analysis, providing data structures and operations for working with structured data
resources.fullDescription: Pandas is a fast, powerful, flexible and easy to use open-source data analysis and manipulation tool, built on top of the Python programming language. It provides data structures and operations for manipulating numerical tables and time series. Pandas is widely used in data science, machine learning, and financial analysis.

specifications.File Formats: CSV, Excel, JSON, SQL, HDF5, Parquet, etc.
specifications.Memory Usage: Efficient memory usage with categorical data
specifications.Integration: Seamless integration with scikit-learn, matplotlib, etc.

bestPractices: Use vectorized operations instead of loops
Set appropriate data types to save memory
Use categorical data for repeated strings
Use method chaining for readable code
Profile memory usage with large datasets

codeSnippet.title: Pandas Data Analysis Example
codeSnippet.code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Create sample data
data = {
    'Name': ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Charlie', 'Diana', 'Eve'],
    'Age': [25, 30, 35, 28, 32],
    'Salary': [50000, 60000, 75000, 55000, 80000],
    'Department': ['Engineering', 'Sales', 'Engineering', 'Marketing', 'Engineering']
}

df = pd.DataFrame(data)
print('Original DataFrame:')
print(df)
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# Data filtering
engineering = df[df['Department'] == 'Engineering']
print('Employees in Engineering:')
print(engineering)
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# Data aggregation
print('Average salary by department:')
dept_avg = df.groupby('Department')['Salary'].mean()
print(dept_avg)
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# Data transformation
df['Experience_Level'] = pd.cut(df['Age'], 
                                bins=[0, 30, 40, 100], 
                                labels=['Junior', 'Mid', 'Senior'])
print('With experience levels:')
print(df[['Name', 'Age', 'Experience_Level']])

resources.name: NumPy - Numerical Computing
resources.shortDesc: Fundamental package for scientific computing with Python, providing powerful N-dimensional array objects and mathematical functions
resources.fullDescription: NumPy is the fundamental package for scientific computing with Python. It provides a high-performance multidimensional array object, and tools for working with these arrays. It's the core library for scientific computing in Python and the foundation for libraries like Pandas, SciPy, and scikit-learn.

specifications.Data Types: int8, int16, int32, int64, float16, float32, float64, complex

bestPractices: Use vectorized operations instead of loops
Use views instead of copies for slicing
Use numpy functions over Python loops
Profile memory usage with large arrays

codeSnippet.title: NumPy Scientific Computing Example
codeSnippet.code:
import numpy as np

# Creating arrays
print('1. Array Creation:')
a = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
print(f'1D array: {a}, shape: {a.shape}')

b = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])
print(f'2D array: {b}, shape: {b.shape}')

# Array operations
print('\n2. Array Operations:')
x = np.array([1, 2, 3])
y = np.array([4, 5, 6])
print(f'Addition: {x + y}')
print(f'Element-wise multiply: {x * y}')
print(f'Dot product: {np.dot(x, y)}')

# Broadcasting
print('\n3. Broadcasting:')
a = np.array([[1, 2, 3], [4, 5, 6]])
b = np.array([10, 20, 30])
print(f'Array a shape: {a.shape}, b shape: {b.shape}')
print(f'Broadcasting result: {a + b}')

# Linear algebra
print('\n4. Linear Algebra:')
A = np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])
print(f'Matrix: {A}')
print(f'Determinant: {np.linalg.det(A):.2f}')
print(f'Inverse: {np.linalg.inv(A)}')

resources.name: Matplotlib - Data Visualization
resources.shortDesc: Comprehensive library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations in Python
resources.fullDescription: Matplotlib is a comprehensive library for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations in Python. It provides an object-oriented API for embedding plots into applications and is the foundation for many other visualization libraries. Matplotlib produces publication-quality figures in a variety of formats and interactive environments.

specifications.Backends: Agg, Cairo, GTK, Qt, Tk, WebAgg, etc.
specifications.Output Formats: PNG, PDF, SVG, EPS, PGF, WebP
specifications.Plot Types: Line, bar, scatter, histogram, box, violin, etc.

codeSnippet.title: Matplotlib Visualization Examples
codeSnippet.code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Set style
plt.style.use('seaborn-v0_8')

# Create sample data
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y1 = np.sin(x)
y2 = np.cos(x)
data = np.random.normal(50, 15, 1000)
categories = ['A', 'B', 'C', 'D', 'E']
values = [23, 45, 56, 78, 32]

# Line plot
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
plt.plot(x, y1, label='sin(x)', color='blue', linewidth=2)
plt.plot(x, y2, label='cos(x)', color='red', linewidth=2, linestyle='--')
plt.title('Line Plot Example')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('y')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.savefig('line_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Bar chart
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
bars = plt.bar(categories, values, color='skyblue')
plt.title('Bar Chart Example')
plt.ylabel('Values')
for bar, value in zip(bars, values):
    plt.text(bar.get_x() + bar.get_width()/2, bar.get_height() + 1, 
             str(value), ha='center', va='bottom')
plt.savefig('bar_chart.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Histogram
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.hist(data, bins=30, alpha=0.7, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Histogram Example')
plt.xlabel('Value')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.savefig('histogram.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

resources.name: Seaborn - Statistical Data Visualization
resources.shortDesc: Statistical data visualization library based on matplotlib, providing a high-level interface for attractive statistical graphics
resources.fullDescription: Seaborn is a Python data visualization library based on matplotlib. It provides a high-level interface for drawing attractive and informative statistical graphics. Seaborn helps you explore and understand your data with beautiful visualizations that make it easy to identify patterns and relationships.

features.Statistical Plots: Box plots, violin plots, swarm plots
features.Relational Plots: Scatter plots, line plots with regression
features.Categorical Plots: Bar plots, count plots, point plots

bestPractices: Use appropriate plot types for data types

codeSnippet.title: Seaborn Statistical Visualization
codeSnippet.code:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Set style
sns.set_style('whitegrid')

# Load sample data
tips = sns.load_dataset('tips')
print('Sample data:')
print(tips.head())
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# Distribution plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.histplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', kde=True, color='skyblue')
plt.title('Distribution of Total Bill Amounts')
plt.savefig('distribution_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Scatter plot with regression
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.regplot(data=tips, x='total_bill', y='tip', 
           scatter_kws={'alpha':0.6}, line_kws={'color': 'red'})
plt.title('Total Bill vs Tip Amount')
plt.savefig('scatter_regression.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Box plot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=tips, x='day', y='total_bill', palette='Set3')
plt.title('Total Bill Distribution by Day')
plt.savefig('box_plot.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

# Heatmap for correlations
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
numeric_cols = ['total_bill', 'tip', 'size']
corr_matrix = tips[numeric_cols].corr()
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', center=0)
plt.title('Correlation Matrix')
plt.savefig('correlation_heatmap.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight')
plt.show()

resources.name: Jupyter Notebook - Interactive Computing
resources.shortDesc: Web-based interactive computing environment for creating and sharing documents with live code, equations, and visualizations
resources.fullDescription: Jupyter Notebook is an open-source web application that allows you to create and share documents that contain live code, equations, visualizations and narrative text. It's widely used for data analysis, machine learning, scientific computing, and educational purposes. Jupyter supports over 40 programming languages including Python, R, Julia, and Scala.

specifications.Languages: Python, R, Julia, Scala, and 40+ others

features.Interactive Code: Execute code in cells with immediate output

bestPractices: Keep cells focused on single tasks

codeSnippet.title: Jupyter Notebook Data Analysis
codeSnippet.code:
# This is a Jupyter notebook cell

# Cell 1: Import libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

print('Libraries imported successfully!')

# Cell 2: Load and explore data
df = pd.read_csv('sample_data.csv')  # In real scenario
print('Data loaded:')
print(df.head())
print(f'\nData shape: {df.shape}')
print(f'Columns: {list(df.columns)}')

# Cell 3: Data visualization
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='feature1', y='feature2', hue='category')
plt.title('Data Visualization in Jupyter')
plt.savefig('jupyter_plot.png', dpi=150)
plt.show()

# Cell 4: Interactive elements
from ipywidgets import interact, widgets

@interact

def plot_histogram(bins=(5, 50, 5)):
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
    plt.hist(df['numeric_column'], bins=bins, alpha=0.7, color='skyblue')
    plt.title(f'Histogram with {bins} bins')
    plt.xlabel('Value')
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')
    plt.show()

print('Interactive histogram created!')

# Cell 5: Markdown documentation
"""
## Analysis Summary

This notebook demonstrates:
1. Data loading and exploration
2. Statistical analysis
3. Data visualization
4. Interactive elements

### Key Findings:
- Dataset contains X records
- Main insights from the analysis
- Recommendations for next steps
"""

resources.name: R Programming Language - Statistical Computing
resources.shortDesc: Programming language and environment for statistical computing and graphics, widely used in data science and research
resources.fullDescription: R is a programming language and free software environment for statistical computing and graphics. It is widely used among statisticians and data miners for developing statistical software and data analysis. R provides a wide variety of statistical and graphical techniques, and is highly extensible through packages.

features.Reporting: R Markdown, knitr for reproducible research

codeSnippet.title: R Statistical Analysis Example
codeSnippet.code:
# Load required libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)

# Create sample data
set.seed(123)
data <- data.frame(
  group = rep(c('A', 'B', 'C'), each = 50),
  value = c(
    rnorm(50, mean = 10, sd = 2),
    rnorm(50, mean = 12, sd = 2),
    rnorm(50, mean = 8, sd = 2)
  )
)

# Basic data exploration
head(data)
summary(data)
str(data)

# Statistical tests
t_test <- t.test(value ~ group, data = data[data$group %in% c('A', 'B'),])
print(t_test)

anova_result <- aov(value ~ group, data = data)
print(summary(anova_result))

# Data visualization
# Histogram
ggplot(data, aes(x = value, fill = group)) +
  geom_histogram(alpha = 0.7, position = 'identity', bins = 20) +
  labs(title = 'Distribution by Group',
       x = 'Value',
       y = 'Count') +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave('histogram.png', width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)

# Box plot
ggplot(data, aes(x = group, y = value, fill = group)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = 'Box Plot by Group',
       x = 'Group',
       y = 'Value') +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave('boxplot.png', width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)

# Scatter plot with regression
set.seed(456)
data2 <- data.frame(
  x = rnorm(100, 50, 10),
  y = 2 * rnorm(100, 50, 10) + rnorm(100, 0, 5)
)

ggplot(data2, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = 'blue') +
  geom_smooth(method = 'lm', color = 'red', se = TRUE) +
  labs(title = 'Scatter Plot with Regression Line',
       x = 'X Variable',
       y = 'Y Variable') +
  theme_minimal()

ggsave('scatter_regression.png', width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 300)

# Linear regression model
model <- lm(y ~ x, data = data2)
print(summary(model))

# Save results
write.csv(data, 'analysis_data.csv', row.names = FALSE)
saveRDS(model, 'linear_model.rds')

resources.name: Tableau - Business Intelligence & Analytics
resources.shortDesc: Leading business intelligence and data visualization platform for creating interactive dashboards and reports
resources.fullDescription: Tableau is a leading business intelligence and data visualization platform that helps people see and understand data. It allows users to connect to various data sources, create interactive dashboards, and share insights across organizations. Tableau is known for its ease of use and powerful visualization capabilities.

specifications.File Formats: Excel, CSV, JSON, databases, cloud services
specifications.Sharing: Tableau Public, Tableau Server, Tableau Online

bestPractices: Use data extracts for better performance
Test with real data before publishing

codeSnippet.title: Tableau Data Connection Example
codeSnippet.code:
-- Sample SQL queries that work well with Tableau

-- 1. Basic data extraction
SELECT 
    order_date,
    customer_region,
    product_category,
    SUM(sales_amount) as total_sales,
    COUNT(DISTINCT customer_id) as unique_customers,
    AVG(sales_amount) as avg_order_value
FROM sales_orders 
WHERE order_date >= '2023-01-01'
GROUP BY order_date, customer_region, product_category
ORDER BY order_date, total_sales DESC;

-- 2. Time series analysis
SELECT 
    DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) as month,
    product_category,
    SUM(sales_amount) as monthly_sales,
    LAG(SUM(sales_amount)) OVER (
        PARTITION BY product_category 
        ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
    ) as prev_month_sales,
    CASE 
        WHEN LAG(SUM(sales_amount)) OVER (
            PARTITION BY product_category 
            ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
        ) IS NOT NULL 
        THEN (SUM(sales_amount) - LAG(SUM(sales_amount)) OVER (
            PARTITION BY product_category 
            ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
        )) / LAG(SUM(sales_amount)) OVER (
            PARTITION BY product_category 
            ORDER BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
        ) * 100 
        ELSE NULL 
    END as growth_rate
FROM sales_orders 
GROUP BY DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date), product_category
ORDER BY month, product_category;

-- 3. Customer segmentation
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    customer_name,
    total_orders,
    total_spent,
    avg_order_value,
    last_order_date,
    CASE 
        WHEN total_spent >= 10000 THEN 'High Value'
        WHEN total_spent >= 5000 THEN 'Medium Value'
        WHEN total_spent >= 1000 THEN 'Low Value'
        ELSE 'New Customer'
    END as customer_segment
FROM (
    SELECT 
        customer_id,
        customer_name,
        COUNT(order_id) as total_orders,
        SUM(order_total) as total_spent,
        AVG(order_total) as avg_order_value,
        MAX(order_date) as last_order_date
    FROM customers c
    JOIN orders o ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
    GROUP BY customer_id, customer_name
) customer_summary
ORDER BY total_spent DESC;

resources.name: Apache Spark - Big Data Processing
resources.shortDesc: Unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing, supporting batch processing, streaming, machine learning, and graph processing
resources.fullDescription: Apache Spark is a unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing. It provides high-level APIs in Java, Scala, Python and R, and an optimized engine that supports general execution graphs. Spark supports batch processing, streaming analytics, machine learning, and graph processing with consistent APIs.

specifications.Components: Spark Core, Spark SQL, Spark Streaming, MLlib, GraphX

features.Spark SQL: SQL and DataFrame APIs for structured data

bestPractices: Use DataFrames over RDDs for better performance
Use broadcast joins for small datasets
Monitor job performance with Spark UI
Use Delta Lake for ACID transactions

codeSnippet.title: Apache Spark Data Processing Example
codeSnippet.code:
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, avg, count, sum, window
import pyspark.sql.functions as F

# Initialize Spark session
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName('DataScienceExample') \
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.enabled', 'true') \
    .config('spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled', 'true') \
    .getOrCreate()

print('Spark version:', spark.version)
print('='*50)

# Create sample data
data = [
    ('Alice', 25, 'Engineering', 75000, '2023-01-15'),
    ('Bob', 30, 'Sales', 60000, '2023-02-20'),
    ('Charlie', 35, 'Engineering', 80000, '2023-01-10'),
    ('Diana', 28, 'Marketing', 55000, '2023-03-05'),
    ('Eve', 32, 'Engineering', 90000, '2023-02-28')
]

columns = ['name', 'age', 'department', 'salary', 'hire_date']
df = spark.createDataFrame(data, columns)

df.show()
df.printSchema()
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# DataFrame operations
# Filtering and aggregation
engineering_df = df.filter(col('department') == 'Engineering')
print('Engineering employees:')
engineering_df.show()

# Group by and aggregate
department_stats = df.groupBy('department').agg(
    count('name').alias('employee_count'),
    avg('salary').alias('avg_salary'),
    avg('age').alias('avg_age')
)
print('\nDepartment statistics:')
department_stats.show()

# Adding new columns
df_with_experience = df.withColumn(
    'hire_year', F.year(F.to_date('hire_date'))
).withColumn(
    'experience_years', 2024 - col('hire_year')
).withColumn(
    'salary_category', 
    F.when(col('salary') >= 80000, 'Senior')
     .when(col('salary') >= 60000, 'Mid')
     .otherwise('Junior')
)

print('\nDataFrame with new columns:')
df_with_experience.select('name', 'salary', 'salary_category', 'experience_years').show()
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# SQL queries
df.createOrReplaceTempView('employees')

sql_results = spark.sql('''
    SELECT 
        department,
        COUNT(*) as employee_count,
        ROUND(AVG(salary), 2) as avg_salary,
        ROUND(AVG(age), 1) as avg_age,
        MAX(salary) as max_salary
    FROM employees 
    GROUP BY department
    ORDER BY avg_salary DESC
''')

print('SQL query results:')
sql_results.show()
print('\n' + '='*50 + '\n')

# Machine Learning with MLlib
from pyspark.ml.feature import VectorAssembler, StringIndexer
from pyspark.ml.classification import LogisticRegression
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import BinaryClassificationEvaluator
from pyspark.ml import Pipeline

# Create sample classification data
ml_data = [
    (25, 75000, 'Engineering', 1),
    (30, 60000, 'Sales', 0),
    (35, 80000, 'Engineering', 1),
    (28, 55000, 'Marketing', 0),
    (32, 90000, 'Engineering', 1),
    (26, 65000, 'Sales', 0),
    (40, 95000, 'Engineering', 1),
    (29, 58000, 'Marketing', 0)
]

ml_columns = ['age', 'salary', 'department', 'high_performer']
ml_df = spark.createDataFrame(ml_data, ml_columns)

# Prepare features
indexer = StringIndexer(inputCol='department', outputCol='dept_index')
assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=['age', 'salary', 'dept_index'], 
    outputCol='features'
)

# Create and train model
lr = LogisticRegression(labelCol='high_performer', featuresCol='features')
pipeline = Pipeline(stages=[indexer, assembler, lr])

model = pipeline.fit(ml_df)

# Make predictions
predictions = model.transform(ml_df)
print('\nMachine Learning predictions:')
predictions.select('age', 'salary', 'department', 'high_performer', 'prediction', 'probability').show()

# Stop Spark session
spark.stop()
print('\nSpark session stopped.')
